source_paper_title: The Complexity of Recognizing Facets for the Knapsack Polytope
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Chen_2025, title={The Complexity of Recognizing Facets for the Knapsack Polytope}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0481}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0481}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Chen, Rui and Zhu, Haoran}, year={2025}, month=nov }
context:
Page 16, Section 5 (Concluding Remarks)

It is still open whether it can be done in time \(f(K)\cdot \mathrm{poly}(n)\) where \(f\) is some computable function independent of \(n\), i.e., whether the knapsack facet recognition problem is fixed-parameter tractable.
title: Fixed-parameter tractability of knapsack facet recognition by distinct coefficients parameter
background_latex:
Let 
\[Q:=\{x\in\{0,1\}^n: a^\top x\le b\}\]
be a (0--1) knapsack set with \(a\in\mathbb{N}^n\) and \(b\in\mathbb{N}\) such that \(\max_i a_i\le b\), and let \(P:=\mathrm{conv}(Q)\) be the associated knapsack polytope, which is then full-dimensional.

Consider a linear inequality
\[\alpha^\top x \le \beta\]
with \((\alpha,\beta)\in\mathbb{Z}^{n+1}_+\) (componentwise nonnegative integers). Let
\[K := |\alpha|_+ := \bigl|\{\alpha_i : \alpha_i>0,\ i\in[n]\}\bigr|\]
be the number of distinct positive coefficient values appearing among \(\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_n\).

Define the decision problem \(\text{KNAPSACK\_FACETS}\): given \((a,b,\alpha,\beta)\) as above, decide whether \(\alpha^\top x\le \beta\) defines a facet of \(P\) (i.e., it is valid for \(P\) and the face \(\{x\in P: \alpha^\top x=\beta\}\) has dimension \(\dim(P)-1\)).

The paper shows that \(\text{KNAPSACK\_FACETS}\) is \(\mathrm{D}^p\)-complete in general, and gives an XP-time algorithm parameterized by \(K\) that runs in time \(n^{K+O(1)}\) (assuming unit-cost arithmetic).
problem_definition_latex:
Design an algorithm that, given a knapsack polytope \(P=\mathrm{conv}(\{x\in\{0,1\}^n: a^\top x\le b\})\) with \((a,b)\in\mathbb{N}^{n+1}\) and an inequality \(\alpha^\top x\le \beta\) with \((\alpha,\beta)\in\mathbb{Z}^{n+1}_+\), decides whether \(\alpha^\top x\le \beta\) defines a facet of \(P\) in time
\[f(K)\cdot \mathrm{poly}(n),\]
where \(K=|\alpha|_+\) is the number of distinct positive coefficient values in \(\alpha\), and \(f\) is a computable function independent of \(n\).

Let 
\[Q:=\{x\in\{0,1\}^n: a^\top x\le b\}\]
be a (0--1) knapsack set with \(a\in\mathbb{N}^n\) and \(b\in\mathbb{N}\) such that \(\max_i a_i\le b\), and let \(P:=\mathrm{conv}(Q)\) be the associated knapsack polytope, which is then full-dimensional.

Consider a linear inequality
\[\alpha^\top x \le \beta\]

\[K := |\alpha|_+ := \bigl|\{\alpha_i : \alpha_i>0,\ i\in[n]\}\bigr|\]
be the number of distinct positive coefficient values appearing among \(\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_n\).

Define the decision problem \(\text{KNAPSACK\_FACETS}\): given \((a,b,\alpha,\beta)\) as above, decide whether \(\alpha^\top x\le \beta\) defines a facet of \(P\) (i.e., it is valid for \(P\) and the face \(\{x\in P: \alpha^\top x=\beta\}\) has dimension \(\dim(P)-1\)).

Design an algorithm that, given a knapsack polytope \(P=\mathrm{conv}(\{x\in\{0,1\}^n: a^\top x\le b\})\) with \((a,b)\in\mathbb{N}^{n+1}\) and an inequality \(\alpha^\top x\le \beta\) with \((\alpha,\beta)\in\mathbb{Z}^{n+1}_+\), decides whether \(\alpha^\top x\le \beta\) defines a facet of \(P\) in time
\[f(K)\cdot \mathrm{poly}(n),\]
where \(K=|\alpha|_+\) is the number of distinct positive coefficient values in \(\alpha\), and \(f\) is a computable function independent of \(n\).
open_problem_quote: It is still open whether it can be done in time \(f(K)\cdot \mathrm{poly}(n)\) where \(f\) is some computable function independent of \(n\), i.e., whether the knapsack facet recognition problem is fixed-parameter tractable.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is an algorithmic-existence question (whether an FPT algorithm exists) rather than a universal inequality that admits small explicit counterexamples. Evidence would likely come from complexity-theoretic reductions or algorithm design, not from searching finite instances.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can represent candidate algorithms as code and benchmark runtime/scaling on families of synthetic instances with fixed \(K\), but the key hurdle is proving worst-case \(f(K)\) dependence or establishing hardness (e.g., W[1]-hardness), which is not directly captured by empirical fitness signals. Still, automated exploration could generate promising dynamic programming formulations or kernelization ideas for small \(K\).

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper establishes that recognizing whether a given nonnegative integer inequality \(\alpha^\top x\le \beta\) defines a facet of the 0–1 knapsack polytope \(P=\mathrm{conv}(\{x\in\{0,1\}^n: a^\top x\le b\})\) is \(\mathrm{D}^p\)-complete, resolving a conjecture of Hartvigsen and Zemel. Despite this hardness, it gives an XP algorithm parameterized by \(K=|\alpha|_+\), the number of distinct positive coefficient values in \(\alpha\), running in \(n^{K+O(1)}\) time by enumerating and rank-testing a structured family of “basic knapsack solutions” induced by grouping variables with equal \(\alpha\)-coefficients.

The open problem asks whether this XP dependence can be improved to fixed-parameter tractability: an algorithm with running time \(f(K)\,\mathrm{poly}(n)\). No forward-citation evidence was provided, so there is currently no identified subsequent work resolving the FPT question. Promising directions suggested by the structure of the XP algorithm include compressing the enumeration of basic solutions via dynamic programming over the \(K\) coefficient blocks, exploiting matroid/representative-set style rank arguments to avoid explicit enumeration, or proving parameterized hardness (e.g., \(\mathrm{W}[1]\)-hardness) by adapting the paper’s \(\mathrm{D}^p\)-hardness reduction to preserve small \(K\).

sources.title: The Complexity of Recognizing Facets for the Knapsack Polytope
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows KNAPSACK_FACETS is \(\mathrm{D}^p\)-complete in general and gives an XP algorithm running in \(n^{K+O(1)}\) time where \(K=|\alpha|_+\) is the number of distinct positive coefficients in \(\alpha\). Leaves open whether the problem is fixed-parameter tractable (FPT) in \(K\).
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the open problem: improve the XP dependence \(n^{K+O(1)}\) to an FPT running time \(f(K)\,\mathrm{poly}(n)\) for deciding whether \(\alpha^\top x\le \beta\) defines a facet of a 0–1 knapsack polytope.
